subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Women Candidates for Senate by Party, 1980–2020
stimulus: Sources: Colorado Public Radio, 2018; Ballotpedia, 2022. Line graph showing Women Candidates for Senate by Party from 1980 to 2020 for Democratic and Republican women candidates in general elections.

parts.prompt: Identify the year with the fewest number of Democratic women candidates for the Senate.

scoring_points.description: Identify the year with the fewest number of Democratic women candidates for the Senate.
wrong_examples: 1980. (Incorrect — 1988 shows a lower value for Democratic women candidates than 1980 on the graph)
1982. (Incorrect — while 1982 was also an early year with few candidates, 1988 is the actual low point)
common_traps: Students often default to the first year on the graph (1980) assuming the trend starts at the bottom — but 1988 is the specific low point for Democratic women Senate candidates
Read the graph carefully for the lowest point on the Democratic line specifically, not for both parties combined

parts.prompt: Describe the difference in an overall trend in Democratic and Republican women candidates for the Senate as illustrated in the graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must describe both trends and note the difference between them — not just describe one party's trend. Accept any accurate description of the difference.

scoring_points.description: Describe the difference in an overall trend in Democratic and Republican women candidates for the Senate as illustrated in the graph.

required_elements: There is an overall increase in female Senate candidates for both Democrats and Republicans, but the increase for Democrats is larger than it is for Republicans.
alternatives.correct_example: There is an overall increase in female Senate candidates for both Democrats and Republicans, but the increase for Democrats is larger than it is for Republicans.

required_elements: There are more female Democratic candidates than female Republican candidates in every election since 1996.
alternatives.correct_example: There are more female Democratic candidates than female Republican candidates in every election since 1996.

wrong_examples: There are more Democratic women than Republican women running for Senate. (Describes the current state but does not describe trends over time or the difference in the pace of change)
The number of women candidates has increased. (Does not describe BOTH parties' trends or note how they differ)
common_traps: The question asks about the DIFFERENCE in trends between the two parties — both parties show increases, but the key observation is that Democrats increased more steeply and surpassed Republicans
A response that only describes one party's trend will not earn full credit — both trends and their contrast must be addressed

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion that explains an overall trend shown in the graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must go beyond describing the trend to drawing a conclusion about why it exists. Accept any plausible conclusion supported by the data.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion that explains an overall trend shown in the graph.

required_elements: The total number of female Senate candidates increased over time because both parties are responding to voters who want to vote for women.
alternatives.correct_example: The total number of female Senate candidates increased over time because both parties are responding to voters who want to vote for women.

required_elements: The number of women candidates for the Senate increases over time, which might be due to how political parties have changed their messaging over time.
alternatives.correct_example: The number of women candidates for the Senate increases over time, which might be due to how political parties have changed their messaging over time.

required_elements: A broader recognition of the role of women in society has led to more women choosing to run for the Senate.
alternatives.correct_example: A broader recognition of the role of women in society has led to more women choosing to run for the Senate.

required_elements: Democrats have been more successful in recruiting female Senate candidates to run for office than Republicans.
alternatives.correct_example: Democrats have been more successful in recruiting female Senate candidates to run for office than Republicans.

wrong_examples: More women are running for Senate now than before. (Describes the trend — does not draw a conclusion about why it exists)
Democrats have more women than Republicans. (Describes a comparison between parties without drawing a conclusion about why or what it means)
common_traps: A conclusion must go beyond describing the trend — it must explain WHY the trend exists or what it means; saying 'the number increased' is description, not conclusion
Connect the trend to a social, political, or institutional cause: changing societal attitudes, party recruitment strategies, or voter demand for diverse representation

parts.prompt: Explain how the data in the graph might affect political participation.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific connection between the data about women candidates and political participation. Accept any plausible explanation.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the data in the graph might affect political participation.

required_elements: Women may be more encouraged to vote when they see female candidates on the ballot.
alternatives.correct_example: Women may be more encouraged to vote when they see female candidates on the ballot.

required_elements: Since there are more women candidates for the Senate than before, political participation might increase because different demographic groups will feel more empowered by the political process.
alternatives.correct_example: Since there are more women candidates for the Senate than before, political participation might increase because different demographic groups will feel more empowered by the political process.

required_elements: Participation may decline among individuals who prefer male candidates for office.
alternatives.correct_example: Participation may decline among individuals who prefer male candidates for office.

wrong_examples: More women running is good for democracy. (Evaluative claim without explaining a specific mechanism connecting women candidates to voter participation rates)
Women candidates attract more donations. (Describes campaign finance, not voter participation — the question asks about political participation such as voting, volunteering, or civic engagement)
common_traps: Political participation includes voting, volunteering for campaigns, donating, contacting elected officials, and running for office — the most common answer is voting behavior
The explanation must draw a causal connection: because more women are running → specific demographic groups feel more represented → they are more likely to participate
